coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanocomposites loaded hydrogels as a theranostic platform for advanced healthcare applications: Recent trends and opportunities
coredata.dc:creator: Sood, Ankur
Das, Sabya Sachi
Dev, Atul
Bhardwaj, Dimpy
Kumar, Anuj
Agrawal, Garima
Han, Sung Soo
coredata.dc:description: With the persistent escalating exigence for new avenues in drug delivery strategies and imaging applications, different novel and efficient nanomaterials have surfaced. The league of these nanomaterials demonstrates many unique properties of which exceptional optical signals are exceedingly investigated. The confinement within the nano range endows exclusive optical attributes that can be explored in the domain of bioimaging and fluorescence-based detections. Despite the innovation in engineering fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) with exceptional properties, still, the appetite to develop highly efficient FNPs with multimodal utility can be perceived. Up recently, hydrogels have been projected as an excellent entrant for biomedical applications, due to their biocompatible and biodegradable nature with excellent absorption capacity along with tunable mechanical features. These hydrogels are capable to provide a favourable microenvironment for cellular growth and proliferation. In this critical review article, FNPs conjugated hydrogels (FH) have been critically evaluated for their role in drug delivery and imaging applications. The article will describe the rationale for the origin of fluorescence in FNPs and will also highlight different fabrication methodologies for their synthesis. Further, the utility of these hybrid FH in terms of drug delivery and imaging will be critically discussed and evaluated in detail. Finally, attention will be given to the limitations of these fascinating hybrid hydrogels that limit their clinical translations followed by their prospects that will be decisive for their clinical implications.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanocomposites loaded hydrogels as a theranostic platform for advanced healthcare applications: Recent trends and opportunities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the persistent escalating exigence for new avenues in drug delivery strategies and imaging applications, different novel and efficient nanomaterials have surfaced. The league of these nanomaterials demonstrates many unique properties of which exceptional optical signals are exceedingly investigated. The confinement within the nano range endows exclusive optical attributes that can be explored in the domain of bioimaging and fluorescence-based detections. Despite the innovation in engineering fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) with exceptional properties, still, the appetite to develop highly efficient FNPs with multimodal utility can be perceived. Up recently, hydrogels have been projected as an excellent entrant for biomedical applications, due to their biocompatible and biodegradable nature with excellent absorption capacity along with tunable mechanical features. These hydrogels are capable to provide a favourable microenvironment for cellular growth and proliferation. In this critical review article, FNPs conjugated hydrogels (FH) have been critically evaluated for their role in drug delivery and imaging applications. The article will describe the rationale for the origin of fluorescence in FNPs and will also highlight different fabrication methodologies for their synthesis. Further, the utility of these hybrid FH in terms of drug delivery and imaging will be critically discussed and evaluated in detail. Finally, attention will be given to the limitations of these fascinating hybrid hydrogels that limit their clinical translations followed by their prospects that will be decisive for their clinical implications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials have engrossed active consideration for technologies involving, bioimaging, assays, diagnostics, etc. due to high sensitivity, versatility, and high contrast [1]. Today fluorescent materials have become an integral part of biomedical applications and healthcare industries due to their active commitment to imaging and sensing. Fluorescent imaging could be characterized in two different types based on the origin of fluorescence. The first category involves the source of fluoresce being an intrinsic species (chlorophyll, NADH, etc.), while in the second category, the source of fluorescent signals is introduced externally to the internal architect (organic dyes, fluorescent nanoparticles, etc.) [2].

The use of traditional organic dyes for fluorescence-based theranostic applications has been confronting concerns that includes cytotoxicity and minimal biocompatibility along with low resolution and sensitivity [3]. Also, in the case of organic dye-based fluorophores, the complexity of the designing process, the involvement of a high volume of fluorophores are some setbacks associated with their use [4]. With the advancement in the field of materials, nanomaterials have surfaced as a compelling preference to be employed for various pharmaceutical and biomedical applications, which include drug delivery, bioimaging, therapeutics, sensing, and diagnosis [5–8]. Moreover, the rising of fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) has become an attractive alternative to replace conventional organic dyes. Within the same excitation parameter, FNPs can deliver a much brighter signal compared to the molecules [5,9]. Constant efforts are underway to understand the chemical and physics considerations that impact the operation of these FNPs.

In contrast to the use of these FNPs alone as dry solids or in solutions, FNPs are being associated with hydrogels to develop fluorescent hybrid hydrogels [10]. Hydrogels offer an excellent platform to be used in various biomedical applications owing to the presence of a three- dimensional (3D) network of interlinked natural or synthetic polymeric chains that are capable of holding a huge amount of water/biological fluids imitating biological tissues [11,12]. Further, the internal architect of hydrogels can be fine-tuned by regulating the water content, 3D network, porous structure, elasticity, etc making hydrogels a convenient and easy- to-handle platform [13].

Up recently, fluorescent hybrid hydrogels have gained considerable attention due to their developmental approach for real-time tracking, delay in signal decay, high contrast, and biocompatibility [14,15]. The functionalization of FNPs with hydrogels renders fluorescent properties to the composite hydrogel and at the same time can be tagged with pharmaceutical agents or biomolecules to attain their therapeutic efficacy apart from being an active bioimaging probe [16]. These smart hydrogels are shaping as a unique class of significant hydrogels that could be used for bioimaging, and can be made stimuli responsive along with multifunctionality [17,18].

In this review, we will summarize the origin of fluorescence in nanoparticles and the factors that affect their luminescent properties. Further, the review highlights the current progress in the synthetic routes of these fluorescent nanoparticles. The review further corresponds to the different functionalization strategies resulting in the formation of fluorescent hybrid hydrogels (FH) (FNPs functionalized hydrogels). This review will provide the effects of the degree of crosslinking and the fluorescent quenching behaviour on the utility of these FH for theranostic applications. Finally, the review will be concluded with by discussing the challenges that needs to be addressed in this fascinating field along with their captivating future. The development of novel strategies and dedicated approaches will further increase the efficiency of FHH and proliferate its widespread utility. An overview of the review is represented in Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence (FL) is a form of photoluminescence (PL) in which radiative recombination of excited photons result in emission in a longer wavelength with an extremely short time delay of ∼ 10-8 s. This difference in the energy between the excitation and emission maximum is called the Stokes shift. Different mechanisms of photon conversion in a material can give rise to downconversion (DC), and upconversion (UC) phenomena. In DC, absorption of a single high-energy photon results in the emission of two or more low-energy photons at longer wavelengths while in upconversion (UC), absorption of two or more low-energy photons results in the emission of a single higher-energy photon. Since, the lower-frequency photons are converted to higher-frequency light through fluorescence, the process is called frequency upconversion. The origin of fluorescence varies in different materials, however, when it comes to the nanoparticles, it could be due to the excitonic recombination in quantum dots, defect-level emission in semiconductor quantum dots and nanoparticles, and transition between rare earth ion levels in phosphors (insulator, doped with a suitable light emitting impurity of rare earth or transition metal) of large bandgap insulators. Metal nanoparticles develop luminescence due to the excitation of d-electrons to the sp-conduction band followed by subsequent radiative recombination, however, the dominance of non-radiative relaxation processes results in their low quantum efficiency.

Chemical synthetic methods include electrochemical synthesis, acidic oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted/ultrasonic treatment, solution chemistry methods, supported synthesis, etc. Among the numerous synthetic methods, electrochemical synthesis has been repeatedly reported over the past decades.

It is thoroughly postulated that upon reduction of transition metal precursors, insoluble powders are produced. However, if this reduction takes place in the presence of certain chemicals that can act as stabilizers, nanosized metal clusters are obtained. Here, the stabilizer’s molecule can envelop the formed metal clusters thereby protecting them against agglomeration/aggregation. This is the central concept behind many chemical synthetic protocols [19]. Electrochemical synthetic methods are mostly used for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots (QDs) because these methods provide excellent properties to nanomaterials which include superior water solubility, inertness, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, and resistance to photobleaching. The process of electrosynthesis of any material is subjected to the electrochemical window of the electrolyte, the materials used for electrode preparation, reducing time, the working temperature, the type of the precursor, additives, and basic electrochemical considerations [20]. Electrochemical methods offer to fabricate nanostructures of varied shapes, sizes, and compositions while offering the advantage to carry out synthesis in a single stage while manoeuvring the experimental conditions [21]. In this regard, Zhao et al. prepared fluorescent carbon nanocrystals (CNCs) by electrooxidation of graphite in aqueous solution. The resulting CNCs exhibit blue and yellow fluorescence with low cytotoxicity and no photobleaching ( Fig. 2 (A)) [22]. Further, Qu et al. developed nitrogen-doped Graphene quantum dots(N-GQDs) with oxygen-rich functional groups with a uniform size of 3–5 nm by electrochemical oxidation of a graphene electrode [23].

The method of electrochemical synthesis offers the advantage of being very cost-effective with high production yield. Furthermore, the opportunity of having both batch and continuous processes is another advantage offered by the electrochemical synthesis methods. Several chemical synthetic routes often require high temperatures for execution even as high as 1000 °C [24] which is not the requirement in the case of electrochemical synthetic routes which can work at room temperatures. While working with the electrochemical approach for nanoparticle production, the amount of liquid waste is remarkably reduced which gives a high production rate [25] as the liquid waste amount increases upon scale-up in the nanoparticle production rate [26]. Nevertheless, electrochemical synthesis methods are also associated with some disadvantages, especially during nanocrystal production. Also, room temperature synthesis could produce unordered nanomaterials [27]. It is further highlighted that the use of conductive electrolytes is a prerequisite for the electrochemical approach. Due to this, the utility of ionic liquid electrolytes is growing, leading to an eco-friendly environment.

Acidic oxidation is the simplest chemical method, which requires oxidation of the material under an acidic environment and results in the formation of fluorescent nanoparticles exhibiting fluorescence in a broad range. In this regard, Mao et al. synthesized water-soluble, multicolour fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by refluxing the candle soot with nitric acid. The prepared CDs photoluminescence spectra have a broad color range, with the emission-peak wavelengths ranging from 415 to 615 nm [28]. Several identical nanomaterials with diameters of 3–4 nm were produced using this approach from a variety of carbon starting materials, including single-walled carbon nanotubes, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and graphite. These nanoparticles exhibit strong yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation and shifted emission peaks as the excitation wavelength is changed [29].

Microwave-assisted synthesis of nanoparticles has been a hot area of research in the field of healthcare or biomedical applications [30,31]. Microwave synthesis involves electromagnetic irradiation and facilitates greener and quicker heating to prepare nanomaterials because of lower energy requirements. While chemical reactions are fast with fewer side products, the current microwave-assisted synthesis approach offers high tolerance to high temperature and pressure, controlled regulation, and reproducibility [32]. In the case of microwave/ultrasound- assisted fabrication of nanoparticles, heating is attained via dipolar polarization and ionic conduction [33]. Here, upon irradiation of the dipoles, the solvent molecules try to align with the oscillations of the magnetic field which leads to heating. The frequency of the magnetic field and the rate of molecules alignment regulates the level of heating. Microwave-assisted synthesis is usually performed by keeping samples under frequencies of 300 MHz to 900 MHz in a microwave reactor. This method provides greater control of morphology, size, and dispersion compared with other methods. Microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials has been explored by many research groups. In this regard, Xiao et al synthesized fluorescent CDs 3–5 nm in diameter from ionic liquids via an economical, rapid, and green microwave-assisted approach [34]. Similarly, Tang et al. developed glucose-derived water-soluble crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an average diameter of 1.65 nm ( ∼ 5 layers) using a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [35]. In another study, Sun et al. fabricated fluorescent carbon nanoparticles using single precursor (ammonium citrate dibasic) for both carbon and nitrogen source ( Fig. 2(B)). Here the obtained carbon nanoparticles showed strong excitation-independent fluorescence in the blue region with a quantum yield of almost 20% [36]. Moreover, Pathak et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur (NSCDs) co-doped carbon dots using thiourea and tris–acetate-ethylenediamine through microwave hydrothermal method. The synergistic presence of nitrogen and sulfur as a dopant provided lucrative multicolour fluorescence properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 57% [37].

Solution chemistry-based chemical synthetic method provides simple and powerful routes to synthesize nanomaterials. They are quite general, allowing the preparation of all kinds of nanomaterials, and usually have the advantages of greater capability and flexibility compared to other synthetic routes. The mechanism of nucleation and growth of nanoparticles in solution is described by many theories like Lamer burst nucleation [38] followed by Ostwald ripening [39]. The mechanism and the parameters governing nucleation, growth, size distribution, and orientation of nanoparticles in solution have been explained in detail in a separate review article by Thanh et al. [40]. Both top-down and bottom-up approach is used for synthesizing nanoparticles in the solution but in the solution state, the bottom-up approach is far more popular especially for inorganic nanoparticles with control over the composition, growth, morphology, and properties [33,41]. The Oxidative condensation of aryl groups has been successfully applied to the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) over the past decades. In this regard, Li et al synthesized stable, size-tuneable, colloidal GQDs with narrow size distribution using a solubilization strategy [42]. Further, Zhu et al. developed hydrophilic carbogenic dots (CDs) with a diameter of 1.5–2.5 nm using mesoporous silica spheres as nanoreactors by using an impregnation method [43]. The resulting photoluminescent CDs were monodispersed, photostable, showed low toxicity, strong blue luminescence properties, and high photoluminescence efficiency of 23%. In another study, Yan et al. prepared bright-yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) using anhydrous citric acid as a carbon source and 2, 3-phenazinediamine as a nitrogen source through a solvothermal method [44]. The Y-CDs displayed an abundance of carboxyl groups on the surface, a fluorescence quantum yield of 24%, high sensitivity, and excellent stability. For the synthesis of colloidal nanoparticles, the parameters governing the solution chemistry (pH, ionic strength, temperature, etc.) have to be regulated precisely in order to attain monodispersed nanoparticles with precise structural orientation having the desired properties. Different FNPs synthesized using chemical methods are summarized in Table 1
.
In general, physical methods of fluorescent nanoparticle synthesis mainly include arc discharge, laser ablation/passivation, and plasma treatment.
Laser ablation

Laser ablation is a physical method for the synthesis of several types of nanoparticles including semiconductor quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, nanowires, and core–shell nanoparticles. Laser ablation means the removal of material from a surface using laser irradiation. In this method, nanoparticles are generated by nucleation and growth of laser-vaporized species in a gaseous or liquid environment ( Fig. 3 (A)). The evaporation rate of the target material is highly dependent on the laser source, wavelength, light absorption efficiency of the target, and the environment for nucleation and growth. In earlier days of nanoparticle synthesis using laser ablation, excimer laser in the UV spectrum was used [51,52], nowadays Q-switch pulsed YAG (Yttrium Aluminum Garnet) is in practice [53–55]. Depending on the target materials, wavelengths of these types of lasers can be switched using nonlinear crystals. In addition to the laser wavelength, the growth environment for nucleation also affects the size of synthesized nanoparticles.

The use of low-pressure background gas is preferable for the synthesis of small-size and monodisperse nanoparticles, while liquid media is used to directly disperse nanoparticles into the liquid phase. As newly synthesized particles are very reactive and prone to oxidation and agglomeration, the choice of dispersion medium is very crucial to achieve good dispersity and high purity of nanoparticles. Despite being one of the finest methods for the synthesis of nanoparticles with high purity, the laser ablation method suffers production yield. Other disadvantage includes high input energy demand and small laser irradiating area on target materials. This method is highly suited for the synthesis of anisotropic nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, nanowire, nanorods etc., and show good compatibility with the aerosol flow systems to produce large quantities of nanomaterials. In this regard, Kaczmarek et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) by nanosecond laser ablation of a graphite target immersed in polyethyleneimine (PEI) and ethylenediamine (EDA). In the case of ablation in PEI, most of the products are associated with carbogenic nanostructures, while in the case of EDA, free fluorescent molecules dominated [56]. Further, Malolepszy et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from graphite targets and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanoparticles performed by the nanosecond laser ablation in polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200) [57]. In a separate study, Nguyen et al. synthesized ultrasmall C-dots by focusing two femtosecond laser pulses into graphite powders with the size of about 400 nm dispersed in ethanol solution. Centrifugation was used to separate larger carbon nanoparticles and the C-dots were obtained from the supernatant [58]. All these studies highlight the use of the laser ablation method as a promising technique for the development of fluorescent nanoparticles.
Arc discharge (AcD)

In arc discharge synthesis, the source material is vaporized through an arc discharge between electrodes followed by condensation, nucleation, and growth of nanoparticles under the influence of gas or liquid ( Fig. 3(B)). It is used to generate metal or metal oxide nanoparticles, CNTs, fullerenes, or graphene film. This method typically produces small quantities of metal nanoparticles but is an important method for the synthesis of CNT and fullerenes, core–shell, and encapsulated structures. Synthesis of fluorescent nanoparticles using AcD is not the prime process, nanoparticles develop as AcD dust of nanotubes, nanowires, and nanorods and need further purification steps at the end. The purification step is a complex process and leads to their low quantum yield and consider as one of the limiting factors in bulk synthesis. In this regard, Xu et al. oxidized the arc-discharge soot with HNO3 to introduce the –COOH group and then extracted the chamber with the NaOH to produce a strong black slurry which was separated by gel electrophoresis into SWCNTs. They finally isolated the fast-moving band of highly fluorescent carbon dots nanoparticles [59]. In addition, Zhang et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots using an easy chemical oxidation approach through arc-synthesized carbon byproducts. Such synthesized CDs possess a strong excitation-independent photoluminescence emission at 502 nm and strong upconversion fluorescence under long wavelength radiation from 900 to 550 nm [60]. In another study, Rao et al. synthesized Boron (B) and Nitrogen (N) doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of average diameter 5–6 nm by a two-step chemical route. In the first step, B and N doped graphenes were prepared by the gas phase arc discharge in the presence of a B/N source, and the second step, chemical scissoring of B/N doped graphene to yield B/N-GQDs [61]. The use of the AcD approach for nanoparticles is not only a fast process but also results in monodispersed nanoparticles.
Plasma treatment

Plasmas are ionized quasi-neutral gases consisting of photons, neutral particles, metastable particles, ions, radicals, and electrons. These plasmas can be artificially generated by waves, lasers, combustion, flames, and even nuclear reactions, though the most common approach for plasma generation is electrical discharge. Low-pressure plasma technology has been widely used in the fabrication of nanomaterials [70], while atmospheric plasma technology or non-thermal atmospheric pressure plasmas is a recent development to synthesize nanomaterials at ambient room conditions. Operation at room temperature results in the production of a high quantity of discharge that can be used to modify the surface of the materials through electrons, ions, excited species, reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, UVs, etc. These discharges generated through the plasma, reduce metal ions in liquid to form metal nanoparticles without the need for any external reducing agents. Thus, this method is also considered a “green” method for nanoparticle synthesis and is considered one of the simplest, most effective, low-cost, and clean methods.

The elimination of toxic chemical components during the fabrication process makes plasma-synthesized nanomaterials relatively more biocompatible and appropriate for biomedical applications. Despite being a highly beneficial method for the synthesis of nanoparticles, the presence of a variety of species, electric fields, radiation, and some unknown non-equilibrium chemistry processes between plasma and nanomaterials are exceptionally complex and difficult to control. Additionally, the homogeneity of synthesized nanomaterials is limited, compared to another standard method. Thus, this process is still a work in progress to understand the nature and use of plasma and nanomaterials combination. The use of a plasma treatment process was done by Gokus et al. who demonstrated that using oxygen plasma could induce strong fluorescence into single-layer graphene [62]. Further, Galluccia et al. developed an effective and green way of producing carbon dots by microplasma process using isopropanol. During the reaction, energetic electrons generated by plasma dissociated the isopropanol and achieved the carbonization of the reactant. Precursor concentrations and operating voltages of the plasma treatment affect the composition and size distribution of carbon dots resulting in the difference in PL emission. In addition, gas–liquid interface microplasma-produced carbon dots possess different functional groups such as C = O, C-O, and OH, which could result in good water solubility of carbon dots leading to intense blue emissions in the range of 365–490 nm [63]. Constant efforts are underway to develop an efficient and effective approach with improvements in the plasma treatment process for fabricating fluorescent nanomaterials. Different FNPs synthesized using physical methods are summarized in Table 2
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization strategies to fabricate fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) conjugated hydrogels (FH)
ce:section.ce:para: Conjugation of FNPs to hydrogel network serves two main functions. The first is to render fluorescent properties to the hydrogels which would be very helpful in the bioimaging applications of the hydrogels, and the second is to improve the innate properties of hydrogels which includes enhancement in mechanical attributes, bioactivity, and biocompatibility. The functionalization strategy to fabricate FH includes either loading the FNPs inside the hydrogel network or by grafting the FNPs on to the surface of hydrogels. The selection of functionalization strategy for FH depends upon many factors which include, the targeted application, surface micro-morphology, the charge density of the hydrogels, the functional groups present inside the hydrogel network, and the free energy.

In the case of loading FNPs inside the hydrogel network, the loading efficiency of hydrogels is very crucial. The level of porosity and the water absorption rate governs the loading efficiency of the hydrogels. Further, the proficiency to fine-tune the overall water content and stiffness of the hydrogels could be explored to achieve maximum loading efficiency for hydrogels. This will also aid in recapitulating the exact environment of biological tissue [69]. Moreover, the efficiency and tenure of the encapsulation of FNPs inside the hydrogel network are also affected by their release behaviour. The overall size of hydrogels is associated with a macroscopic length scale ranging from microns to millimetres, whereas the mesh size of the polymer network is associated to a nanometre length scale. Both the length scales play a very crucial role in governing the kinetics of the release behaviour of loaded drugs/FNPs/biomolecules from the hydrogel network.

While the distance to be travelled by the molecules/drug/FNPs to reach the hydrogel surface and get released into the surroundings is controlled by the gel size, the rate of diffusion of these molecules inside the hydrogel network is regulated by the mesh size. If the mesh size is much larger than the hydrodynamic radius of the loaded molecule, drug diffusion is the main mode of drug movement. However, for small molecules whose hydrodynamic radius is very small compared to the mesh size, the diffusion of the loaded molecules is independent of the diffusion [70]. At the molecular and atomistic scale, there could be various chemical interactions occurring between biomolecules/drugs/FNPs and the polymeric chains of the hydrogels. These polymeric chains can possess numerous sites where the loaded biomolecules could bind and interact affecting the overall encapsulation efficiency of the loaded molecules. FNPs could also serve as multifunctional cross-linkers for the hydrogel network with two or more FNPs could be bridged using hydrogel network chains [71]. This loading of FNPs and formation of FNPs conjugated hydrogels largely relies on the affinity of FNPs to interact with the hydrogel network.

When grafting FNPs on to the surface of the hydrogels, the functional groups present on the hydrogel surface play a decisive role in terms of the level of conjugation of FNPs to the hydrogel surface. This attachment could range from covalent conjugation to secondary interactions (ionic or hydrophobic) in nature depending on the availability of the functional groups on the hydrogel surface and the fabricated FNPs, the surface charge on the gel, and FNPs. The strategy of grafting the FNPs to the surface of hydrogels is very important where the FNPs to be conjugated are very small like the nanoclusters of noble metals, as they will be quickly released from the hydrogel networks. In the case of covalent linkage, the fabricated FNPs are tightly bounded to the hydrogel surface through strong bonding strategies including amine bond formation, thiol-ene chemistry, and metal click chemistry. The covalently linked FNPs could be restricted from immediate release which would be very helpful in their long-term usage for bioimaging applications. It is to be noted that, programable covalent linkage can also be explored in order to cleave FNPs at a particular site while taking advantage of the microenvironment of the targeted site. In the case of ionic interactions, the surface charge plays a decisive role and could offer the advantage of nonspecific attachment of FNPs to the hydrogel surface enabling controlled and simultaneous attachment of multiple fluorescent probes and molecules to the hydrogel surface [72]. Here, the attached FNPs are released from the hydrogel surface only when the hydrogel is degraded or the ions present in the microenvironment tend to screen the ionic interaction of the attached FNPs. Further, sometimes hydrophobic natured FNPs upon attachment to the hydrogel surface may disturb the stability, leading to deterioration of the hydrogel network. To tackle this situation, hydrophobic domains and linkers are introduced to the network and surface of the hydrogels which serve as the conjugating sites for FNPs/ molecules attachment. It is to be noted that chemical modifications of hydrogels to explore hydrophobic FNPs attachment may result in efficient interaction but could also impact the biocompatibility and bioactivity of the hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) conjugated hybrid hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels have been proven as an ideal delivery of therapeutics or nanoparticles with improved controlled release behaviour. Studies have reported that injectable hydrogels are mainly used to deliver photoactive therapeutics or nanoparticles efficiently [73]. Precision delivery of theranostic agents to the tumor site is essential to improve their diagnostic and therapeutic efficacy and concurrently minimize adverse effects during treatment [74].

The application of nanoparticles as a single platform for a multifunctional approach has always been the force that motivates many researchers. Utilizing a single platform or system for both biosensing and carrier for delivering therapeutic molecules has been an attractive option. In this regard, Salem et al. characterized protein aggregation using hydrogel-loaded label-free fluorescent nano biosensors. Researchers applied mathematical modelling to identify the effects of various hydrogel properties over the separation of UV-induced IgG1 samples. The nano biosensors potentially detected the existence of higher molecular weight protein species at even at least a concentration of 1% [79]. Alkekhia et al. synthesized hydrogels using Michael-type addition reactions, including polymerization of maleimide-functionalized β-lactamase-cleavable cephalosporin (cross-linker) and thiol-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) macromers (Fig. 5 ). Interestingly, the results of in vitro and ex vivo studies demonstrated that β-lactamases and β-lactamase-producing bacteria deteriorated the hydrogels composed of crosslinkers. Further, the fluorescent polystyrene nanoparticles (model cargo)-incorporated hydrogels exhibited controlled release behaviour based on β-lactamase-responsive effects [80].

In another study, researchers modified the poly(γ-glutamic acid) biopolymer-based hydrogels, synthesized from Bacillus subtilis species, using cholesterol and further used delivery of doxorubicin (DOX), forming raspberry-like hydrogels (RBSs). The hybrid RBSs exhibited pH-dependent response with improved controlled drug release both in vitro and ex vivo. Further, fluorescence-conjugated particles showed high uptake of RBSs into the treated HeLa cells and exert cytotoxic effects in a concentration-and time-dependent manner [81]. Further, Conde et al. prepared hybrid hydrogels composed of gold nanoparticles and 5-fluorouracil labelled with a dark quencher and NIR dye to overcome tumor resistance by silencing the multidrug resistance protein 1 (MRP1). Further, the hybrid hydrogels were conjugated with DNA hairpin to attain the targeted release of the chemotherapeutic drug. The hybrid hydrogels exhibited superior cytotoxicity against triple-negative breast cancer in vivo [82]. In another study, De et al. developed polyacrylic acid (PAA) and polyitaconic acid (PIA) based nanoparticles of sizes ranging between 50 nm using reverse micro-emulsion polymerization methods for potential ocular delivery of brimonidine (BN). The loading efficiency of PAA-BNNs and PIA-BNNs ranged between 80 and 85% and 65–70%, respectively, and showed the drug showed a pH-dependent response. The PAA-BNNs were biocompatible against human corneal epithelial cells incubated, while PIA-BNNs exhibited toxicity. Further, the fluorescently labelled-PAA-BNNs showed supreme adhesiveness towards the corneal (human) surface [83].

In all these studies, the release of chemotherapeutic drugs from the designed system has been influenced by the size of the nanoparticles. The potential interaction of cells with nanoparticles gets a new direction when tagged with hydrogel networks. However, the toxicity evaluation and clearance of the nanoparticles have always been a matter of great interest. Therefore, a multitude of materials and substances are currently under investigation for their utility in numerous biomedical applications.

Over the past two decades, stimuli-responsive drug delivery has been explored by many research groups [84,85]. There are studies where advanced delivery systems are formulated in which either the nanoparticles are stimuli responsive [86,87] or hydrogels encapsulating nanoparticles are made stimuli-responsive [14]. In this regard, Dong et al. reported the fluorescence ability of porphyrin-conjugated PEG-PCL copolymer-based hydrogel systems. Further, they developed thermosensitive porphyrin (fluorescence tag) conjugated PEG-PCL nanogels, which were tracked and delivered to the tissues through intravenous injection. In vitro and in vivo results showed that the nanogel were biocompatible, safe, and targeted tumor tissues with potential fluorescence bioimaging [88]. In another study, Kim et al. developed PLGA NP-based thermoresponsive gels for the intratympanic (IT) delivery of dexamethasone (DM; model drug). DM-NPs showed a particle size and zeta potential of 150.0 ± 3.2 nm and −18.7 ± 0.6, respectively. The DM-NP gels exhibited a good gelatin time of 64 s (37 °C) and exhibited potential cytotoxicity against human melanoma (SK-MEL-31) cells with no significant inflammatory responses in mouse cochlea after IT delivery. In addition, the biodistribution studies of coumarin-6-labeled DM-NP-gel using fluorescence imaging showed sustained release behaviour [89]. In this line, Eljarrat-Binstock et al. assessed the diffusion of charged (cathodal and anodal) fluorescent nanoparticles (20–45 nm) within the rabbit eyes (cornea and sclera) via polyacrylic hydrogel iontophoresis (current intensity of 1.5 mA; 5 min). The in vivo fluorescence intensity studies showed high fluorescence indications in the anterior and posterior optic tissues. In addition, the cationic particles showed better permeability than the anionic particles in the inner ocular tissues. Thus, such systems can be potentially applicable for the sustained and targeted release of drugs with high-quality bioimaging [90]. Further, Fan et al. developed polymeric (polylactic acid) nanoparticle-based thermosensitive hydrogels composed of DOX and LL37 peptides for treating colorectal-peritoneal carcinoma. The hydrogels were administered intraperitoneally and exhibited superior anti-cancer activity against HCT116 cells and in vivo in tumor-induced mice [91]. In another study, Zhu et al. developed a fluorescent pH-sensitive nanogel that applied FRET for detecting the variations in the probe separation and swelling of nanogel within the hydrogels through photoluminescence spectroscopic technique. In this study (9-phenanthryl) methylmethacrylate (Ph) and (9-anthryl) methacrylate (An) were used as the donor and acceptor fluorophore, respectively. These fluorophores were copolymerized with methylmethacrylate (MMA), methacrylic acid (MAA), and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) in order to fabricate NGPh/An particles ( Fig. 6
). Results of small-angle neutron scattering (SANS) for the nanogels indicated the presence of a core–shell configuration with an enflamed mesh size of ∼1.0 nm. Thus, it was hypothesized that the nanogels within the polymeric system showed similar variation in scattering with the increase in the pH as that of only nanogels [92].

Further, Lim et al. fabricated a triple-layered magnetite/quantum dots/hydrogel-based system that exhibited multifaceted biological activities, including magnetic, pH-sensitive, and fluorescence. Initially, magnetic silica nanospheres were synthesized using a sol–gel reaction and the formed nanospheres were then incorporated into poly (N-isopropylacrylamide-co-acrylic acid) hydrogels. Later, the hybrid hydrogels were attached by quantum dots using molecular linking of bi-functional diamines [93]. In another study, Guo et al. investigated the in vivo drug release, delivery manner, and antitumor potentials of 2-methoxyestradiol-loaded and fluorescence-labelled solid lipid nanoparticles (2-MSLNs), further entrapped in the hydrogel system. The results demonstrated that the 2-MSLNs were actively delivered from the hydrogels into the tumorous and carcinogenic cells in a controlled release manner and followed zero-order kinetics. 2-MSLNs-treated BALB/c mice treated with syngeneic 4 T1 breast cancer cells exhibited higher tumor growth suppression than the free drug-treated animals [94].

The internalization of nanoparticles holds great importance in terms of their utility as a carrier for therapeutic molecules. Further, upon reaching the target site, the release pattern of drugs/nanoparticles encapsulated inside the internal architect of hydrogels is very critical. Efforts are being made to design systems in which the release pattern could be regulated. This will provide an opportunity for long-term use of a single system with minimal nuisances to the patients, thus will have high clinical translations.

Nature has always inspired researchers to fabricate materials/substances that have minimal toxicity due to the involvement of minimum chemical agents, and at the same time are in abundance, thus making their availability without much effort. In this regard, Hao et al. fabricated thermosensitive in situ hydrogels composed of Resina Draconis-loaded SLNs via melt-emulsion ultrasonication technique and entrapped them within poloxamer-based hydrogels. In vitro corneal permeation studies established sustained drug release behaviour. The hen's egg test-chorioallantoic membrane (HET-CAM) study established the non-irritating behavior of the formulation. Additionally, the coumarin-6-labeled formulation showed greater homogeneous fluorescence with a deeper permeation intensity into the cornea at various time intervals [95]. In another study, Henke et al. examined the aptness of PEG-based hydrogels for delivering NPs through radically cross-linking oligo-(PEG-fumarate) in the presence of two cross-linkers. Moreover, the cross-linked hydrogels were loaded with various fluorescent nanoparticulate probes, which indicated a strong dependency in the release pattern based on the type of PEG prepolymers used [96]. Further, Heo et al. revealed that the photo-treatable gelatin hydrogel co-loaded with gold nanoparticles (GNPs) exhibited a constructive effect on the osteogenic variation of mesenchymal stem cells and MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-type cells. Results of in vitro studies demonstrated the hydrogels-GNPs promoted proliferation, differentiation, and alkaline phosphate activities in the treated human adipose-derived stem cells in a dose-dependent manner. Additionally, the in vivo results revealed that hydrogels-GNPs (higher concentration) exhibited a significant impact on forming new bones [97]. In another study, Pandey et al. synthesized aqueous soluble and blue fluorescence-producing carbon dots (2–3 nm; CDs) using a DNA precursor. The CDs showed high penetrability in the infective fungal cells, negligible toxicity in healthy cells, good biostability, and biocompatibility. In addition, CDs showed good bioimaging abilities in the treated Candida albicans pathogens. The fluorescent CDs and hydrogel, after appropriate conjugation or payload with biomolecules and drugs, hold superior potential for further biomedical applications, including targeted drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, and wound healing [98].

Natural polymers have always attracted researchers to develop nanoparticles-based drug delivery systems [99]. In this regard, Rakhshaei et al. fabricated carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-GQDs loaded hydrogels through a film-casting technique that showed high biodegradability and biocompatibility. The hydrogels exhibited pH-responsive swelling and deprivation with enhanced tensile strength. In addition, the hydrogels triggered in the fluorescence medium released the model chemotherapeutic drug (DOX) and exhibited potential anticancer activity against human colon adenocarcinoma (HT29) cells [100]. Further, Tran et al. studied the theranostic potential of FITC-labelled BSA hydrogel NPs co-loaded with tetramethylrhodamine-dextran (TTD; model drug and dye) in the skin of porcine ears. The results ensued deeper permeation of TTD in the follicular region of fluorescent signals conforming to TTD as contrasting to fluorescence conforming to the FITC-labeled NPs [101]. In another study, Wang et al. developed a smart hydrogel system composed of nanosized DOX-indocyanine green matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) to achieve combined effects of both chemo- and phototherapy in antitumor therapy. The drug-loaded hydrogel exhibited uniform size distribution, brilliant size constancy, and sustained release behaviour in the existence of MMP-2. In addition, the hydrogel demonstrated supreme photosensitivity properties in the presence of light. Also, hydrogels with NIR (808 nm) irradiation efficiently repressed the viability, incursion, and metastasis of SCC-15 in vitro, and a similar antitumor efficacy was observed in in vivo (intertumoral administration) studies [102]. Moreover, Wang et al. fabricated Mag-loaded zein-based NPs coated with chondroitin sulfate (M−CS−ZNPs) with the particle size of 142.27 ± 5.11 nm. The M−CS−ZNPs showed substantial macrophage-targeting and improved uptake capacity within the colon epithelial cells [103]. Further M−CS−ZNPs were embedded in to hydrogel microspheres using an electrospraying technique, which showed sustained release of the drug. Results of ex vivo and in vivo imaging fluorescence experiments (DiR as fluorescent probe) confirmed the extended colon retention of the drug through modified hydrogels than the M−CS−ZNPs. In addition, the modified hydrogel downregulated the expression levels of both pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β) and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10), confirming anti-colitis response in the Ulcerative Colitis mice model ( Fig. 7
). In a separate study, Xu et al. developed a berberine-encapsulated-hyaluronic acid-modified-L-cysteine-grafted (N-isopropylacrylamide) (BH-Cys-P) hydrogel system. Further, the BH-Cys-P hydrogel was attached with CD44-HA to attain targeted delivery to the carcinogenic cells. Results showed that the hybrid hydrogel system exhibited dual behaviour of drug targeting as well as imaging to achieve synergistic tumor tissue therapy [104].

In order to design an efficient system of hydrogel loaded with nanoparticles for biomedical applications, the response of cellular activity is very crucial. Upon interaction, the cells should proliferate and should be able to maintain health cellular activity. The loading of drug and nanoparticles in a hydrogel system not only provide moist surroundings for the cells to proliferate but also provides a microenvironment similar to the native tissue. It is to be noted that not only cellular internalization but the fate of the nanoparticles inside the cell is very important. Efforts are being made in the line of understanding the fate of different fluorescent nanoparticles inside a cell. The response of cellular activity is a function of the transfer of energy of these fluorescent nanoparticles could provide a thorough understanding of their clinical utility.

The unique properties of hydrogels have given a platform that can be used for numerous biomedical applications [105]. With fluorescent nanoparticles being encapsulated or tagged to these hydrogel networks, a key aspect that has surfaced is the imaging application of these hybrid hydrogels. In this regard, Aigner et al. designed various perylene bisimide (PB) probes composed of oligo-guanidine conjugates and cationic hydrogel NPs for the detection of intracellular pH within 2D adherent mammalian cells and 3D neurosphere cellular models using fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM). The NPs-PB probe demonstrated stable pH calibration and lifetime variations from 4.7 to 3.7 ns between pH 4.4–8 accredited to photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The probes exhibited faster cell permeation and bright staining, but their calibration was significantly affected due to the unreliable microenvironment for quantitative FLIM [106]. In another study, Bakalova et al. developed quantum dots (QD705)-loaded nanohydrogel (QNH), and manganese-QD705-loaded nanohydrogel (Mn-QNH) and confirmed their passive delivery through electroporation technique in the solid tumors in colon cancer-induced mice. Fluorescent imaging was applied to confirm the efficient delivery of the formulation into solid tumors. They reported that implanting QD and QD-like hard materials in nanohydrogel alters the physical characteristics (reduction of size and negative charge and shape variations) of the soft material and changes its pharmacodynamics [107]. Further, Dong et al. designed a potential chemo-immunotherapeutic hydrogel-based system composed of dual-fluorescence imaging-directed co-loaded with DOX and cytosine-phosphate-guanine (CPG) nanoparticles for effective regulation of tumor microenvironment. Compared to pure DOX, hybrid hydrogels established a more permanent immune modulatory effect with possible chemo-immunotherapy mechanisms confirmed through a fluorescence imaging-guided approach [108]. These studies reflect the potential of fluorescent nanoparticle-based hydrogels for therapeutic as well as imaging applications in oncology research. In order to detect the tumor lesions at an early stage, the imaging of the tumor region is very important. The penetration depth and the efficiency of the designed imaging probe plays an important role in to further deciding the fate and effectiveness of the probe being used.

In a separate study, Dong et al. designed a self-crosslinked hydrogel system composed of CPG NPs and IR820 with a dual self-fluorescent effect that exerts photothermal and immunotherapeutic potential. IR820-loaded hydrogels showed potential effects for hyperthermia leading to the elimination of primary tumor by complete exposure to the photothermal-triggered tumor antigens for supported immunotherapy. In addition, CpG NPs significantly enhanced the immune responses of the ancillary against melanoma [109]. Moreover, Ghosh et al. synthesized red-colored copper nanoclusters (Cu-NCs), exhibiting high fluorescence in the aqueous medium comprising poly-(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) and di-hydrolipoic acid [110]. The solid Cu-NCs exerted high stability and retained their optical effects for over 30 days. Further, PVP-stabilized NCs, when converted into hydrogel by cross-linking with poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) ( Fig. 8 (I)), exhibited anticancer activity against cervical cancer (HeLa) cells by inducing apoptosis. The fluorescence spectra of the designed NCs were recorded which also showed high photo stability. In addition, the Cu-NCs emitted red colour that was utilized for optical imaging and flow cytometry assay. The cellular internalization of the designed nanoclusters showed the corresponding emission in the red and green channels. Here, the excitation wavelength in the range of 465–495 nm (blue) and 540/25 nm (green) corresponds to emission wavelength in green (515–555 nm) and red (605/55 nm), respectively ( Fig. 8 (II)).

Förster (or Fluorescence) Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is an exclusive phenomenon in creating fluorescence signals that are sensitive to molecular conformation, association, and separation well within the range of 1–10 nm [111]. FRET has been explored for imaging in healthcare applications. In this regard, Huang et al. developed injectable, thermoresponsive, and poly(ε-caprolactone-co-1,4,8-trioxa[4.6]spiro-9-undecanone)-poly(ethylene glycol)-poly(ε-caprolactone-co-1,4,8-trioxa[4.6]spiro-9-undecanone) (PECT) NP-self-assembled hydrogel co-loaded with DOX (model drug) in peritumoral chemotherapy. The hydrogels exhibited sustained drug release behavior, and further, the drug retention and release behavior of NPs in vivo was confirmed through FRET imaging. In addition, the hydrogels exhibited potential anticancer activity in HepG2 cells with minimal adverse effects [112]. Further, the retention and distribution of DOX was analyzed using fluorescence imaging where an increased level of DOX accumulation was observed in the case of tumors in comparison with other major tissues post 3 hrs of intravenous administration. The retention of DOX was decreased post 24 and 72 h, demonstrating its expulsion from the tumor (Fig. 9 ).

In another study, Jalani et al. reported the controlled drug delivery of biomolecules (large) and deeper tissue imaging abilities of FITC-BSA encapsulated chitosan-based hydrogels conjugated with LiYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+@SiO2 cross-linked with PhL (photocleavable cross-linker). The hybrid hydrogel formulation exhibited a theranostic platform for bio-mapping, tissue engineering, and cell imaging applications [113]. Moreover, Ji et al. designed ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes to precisely sense adenosine triphosphate and bioimaging in living cells based on silica NPs and a DNA-functionalized hydrogel. Notably, the detection method through ratiometry could indeed evade false-positive signals. Moreover, the hydrogels exhibited superior biocompatibility, stability, and high cell permeability with robust responses in living cells [114]. In another study, Jin et al. described the sustained chemo-photothermal activity of an injectable nanosized hydrogel comprised of polypeptide (PC10A), Ag2S-QDs, and paclitaxel. The in vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity results demonstrated that hybrid nanogels suppressed SKOV3 ovarian carcinogenic tumor and exhibited excellent biocompatibility. Interestingly, the nanogels exhibited dual application with improved combinatorial effects, including chemo- and single-NIR fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging in cancer therapy [115]. Further, Zhang et al. reviewed and reported the bioimaging and fluorescent imaging application of cellulose-based nanocarriers. The lower toxicity, biodegradable, biocompatible, and cell membrane permeation of nano-cellulose make them a potential bioactive agent, which can be improved into fluorescent nanoprobes for in vivo bioimaging and cellular tracing cell [116]. Biocompatibility, rapid clearance, release kinetics, biodegradability, and minimal dosage are some of the aspects that need to be addressed while designing fluorescence nanoparticles as a probe for imaging. Once associated with hydrogels, these probes need to have a high quantum yield with minimal signal loss over time. All these studies have reported these characteristics explored for bioimaging applications.

In oncology research, the delivery of therapeutic agents is very important. Strategies are developed to explore the different conditions of tumor microenvironment compared to normal tissue. This may include lower pH, temperature difference, high efflux rate, presence of specific receptors (unique of abundance), etc. In order to explore these conditions, Zhang et al. developed a thermoresponsive and injectable gambogic acid (GA) NP-loaded hydrogels comprising silk fibroin, hydroxypropyl cellulose, and glycerol (Fig. 10 ). The hydrogels exhibited shorter gelling time, good biocompatibility, and sustained release behavior. In addition, gambogic acid (GA) NP-loaded hydrogels and tumor-permeating peptide iRGD, combinedly, showed enhanced anti-tumor activity in gastric cancer with negligible systemic toxicity and improved fluorescence intensity [117].

Further, the ability of metal ions complexes with fluorescent DNA fragments has been studied by Meng et al. in imaging applications. In this study, the researchers incorporated DNA-capped gold NPs (DNA-GNPs) and their corresponding fluorescent DNA arrangements within a porous three-dimensional hydrogel network. This hydrogel network was further loaded with hairpin-locked DNAzyme strands and metal ions (DNA-GNPs M
n
+-H) for instantaneously bio-imaging of complex miRNAs in the living cells. The precise miRNAs activated the strand-shift reaction after transfection within the living cells and consecutively activated the DNAzyme-mediated targeted reprocessing, which increased the fluorescence intensity for bioimaging, even at very lesser expression levels [126]. Yang et al. synthesized spherical polymeric NPs composed of hyperbranced polyethylenimine (hPEI) and 6-hydroxy-2-naphthaldehyde (HNA) using a Schiff base reaction. hPEI-HNA probes showed swift and robust detection of copper ions (Cu2+)within the range of 0–60 μM and a detection limit of 243 nM. The hPEI-HNA probes, due to their excellent optical imaging properties, were used for bioimaging of the living cells ensuing outstanding cell permeability and negligible toxicity [127]. In another study, Yoon et al. synthesized polymer/protein-based hybrid nanogels composed of indocyanine green, polyacrylamide nanoparticles, and human serum albumin. Further, the F3-Cys peptide (targeting moiety), was linked with the nanogel system for targeted delivery to specific cancerous cell lines. The optical imaging of the nanogel was demonstrated in the rat gliosarcoma cell line (9L) using fluorescence imaging. In addition, the photoacoustic activity of the polymeric nanocarriers (applied as image-contrasting agents), was noticed within the range 700–800 nm [128]. Further, Sun et al. synthesized CMC-altered covalent organic framework-LZU1 hydrogel for in situ growth of palladium NPs (Pd-NPs/CMC-COF-LZU1). The resulting hydrogel system showed greater catalytic activity for the conversion of N-butyl-4-NHAlloc-1,8-naphthalimide (NNPH) into N-butyl-4-amido-1,8-naphthalimide (NPH), indicated through changes in colour and fluorescence. The conversion of NNPH into NPH aided the recognition system to accomplish multicolour imaging as well as the count of carcinogenic HeLa cells [129]. While incorporating the fluorescent NPs in the hydrogel network, the solubility of the NPs plays a decisive role, as it will affect their encapsulation and uniform distribution in the hydrogel internal architect. Also, the fluorescent quenching parameters and conditions of the NPs have to be carefully analyzed before incorporating them inside the hydrogel network. This ensures enhanced detection sensitivity, and high efficiency of the designed fluorescent nanoparticles encapsulated hydrogels. Table 3 summarizes the applications of fluorescent nanoparticle conjugated hydrogels (FH) in drug delivery and imaging.

Nanoparticles based systems have found clinical translations and have influenced drug delivery and imaging applications to a greater extent. The state-of-the-art development in fluorescent nanosystems has bring many essential treatment modalities to the field of oncology research. Not only do these fluorescent nanomaterials provide carrier options for therapeutic molecules but have also been widely explored for imaging and sensing applications. Up recently, a substantial amount of research has been done in designing hydrogels to address the clinical and pharmacological issues by being a platform for drug delivery and tissue engineering. A combination of fluorescent nanoparticles with hydrogels has revealed a multitude of approach, that can be exercised for precise drug delivery, tissue targeting ability, and efficient imaging with minimal requirements. The current review summarizes the different methodology to design fluorescent nanomaterials and has comprehensively discussed the advantages and disadvantages of these methods. This review also confers the various investigations that have been reported in the field of fluorescent nanoparticles encapsulated in hydrogels.

Given the different aspects that have been brought forward through breath-taking research in the field of drug delivery and imaging, it would not be embellish to mention that the effectiveness and the clinical relevance of any designed system for theranostic applications is highly dependent on their cellular interactions. However, there is still a lack of a comprehensive understanding of many novel nanoparticle interactions with biological systems.

Furthermore, significant progress has been made in designing hybrid hydrogels. However, the clinical translation of many hybrid hydrogels is lacking due to many challenges that still need improvement and thinking. Gelation of hydrogels sometimes has a very low-temperature window and is too particular and precise. This sometimes is difficult to handle while working with specific pathological conditions. Efforts should be made to widen the temperature-dependent gelation window of hydrogels. In general, hydrogels are crosslinked outside the body and then introduced inside the body. Innovative strategies could be devised where the crosslinking agents could be triggered inside the body to develop in situ hydrogels.

The development of fluorescent nanoparticles has given a boon on the bioimaging front and their association with hydrogels has given a platform for precise imaging and targeted drug delivery approach. However, fluorescence quenching has always been an issue while taking advantage of these fluorescent nanomaterials. Additionally, the narrow excitation window and the transfer of energy upon their interaction with biological systems are some of the hot areas that could still be explored in order to further mature these fascinating materials. Progress in the field of microfluidics has given us a platform to precisely imitate the physiological behaviour of many human organs. This possibility could be explored to further strengthen the utility of these hybrid hydrogels. The contribution of fluorescent nanoparticles tagged hydrogels in biomedical applications is unparallel and a close collaboration between researchers from biological prospectives, material sciences, polymer chemistry, and nanotechnology with an interdisciplinary approach is necessary to furthermore this field from lab scale to clinical translation.
Data availability statement
No new data were created or analysed in this study.
